# SEER-PEPH Developer Guide
Updated for current implementation state

## 1. Purpose of this package

This package is being developed to fit joint treatment-survival models for SEER-Medicare colorectal cancer data, with a current emphasis on validating the survival component on simulated data before moving to the full joint model.

The current state of the project is:

- data preprocessing is implemented
- graph construction is implemented
- simulation is implemented
- model-data assembly is implemented
- several survival model variants have been explored and validated on simulated data
- the current leading survival model is a spatial piecewise-exponential survival model with:
  - baseline covariate effects
  - a delta-only post-treatment effect parameterization
  - spatial frailty
- treatment-process modeling and the full joint treatment-survival model remain the next major development stage

The package goal is not just to fit models, but to support a reproducible workflow with:

- simulation-based validation
- inference wrappers
- prediction under counterfactual treatment times
- diagnostics and calibration
- a clear path to real-data analysis

---

## 2. Current package architecture

The current architecture is organized around the following modules.

### 2.1 `seer_peph.data.prep`

This module handles preprocessing and long-format expansion.

Responsibilities:

- load and encode wide patient-level data
- create model-ready columns such as:
  - time in months
  - treatment time in months
  - binary stage and sex indicators
  - area identifiers
- expand survival outcomes into long piecewise-exponential format
- expand treatment-time outcomes into long piecewise-exponential format

Current survival expansion includes:

- baseline survival interval index `k`
- post-treatment interval index `k_post`
- treatment indicator in the long data `treated_td`
- interval exposure time

Conceptually, this is where the continuous-time problem is converted into a Poisson regression representation.

### 2.2 `seer_peph.data.model_data`

This module converts the long data frames into the array contract used by NumPyro models.

Responsibilities:

- extract arrays for:
  - survival outcomes
  - treatment outcomes
  - design matrices
  - interval indices
  - area indices
- attach graph information:
  - `node1`
  - `node2`
  - `scaling_factor`
  - number of areas `A`
- enforce shape and consistency checks

This is the interface layer between pandas-based preprocessing and JAX/NumPyro-based inference.

### 2.3 `seer_peph.graphs`

This module handles spatial graph construction and validation.

Responsibilities:

- build synthetic graphs for simulation studies
- represent adjacency structure
- compute graph scaling factors for ICAR/BYM2-style priors
- validate graph consistency

Synthetic development has used deterministic ring lattices as the main graph family.

### 2.4 `seer_peph.validation.simulate`

This module generates simulated joint treatment-survival data.

Responsibilities:

- simulate baseline covariates
- simulate treatment timing
- simulate survival outcomes
- simulate shared and process-specific spatial frailties
- store truth columns for later validation

The simulator supports user-controlled truth settings for:

- survival coefficients
- treatment coefficients
- treatment-history effects
- spatial dependence parameters
- spatial variance parameters

This module is central to the package’s validation strategy.

### 2.5 `seer_peph.models`

This module contains NumPyro model definitions.

Several variants have been explored. The current active survival formulation is the delta-only spatial survival model with independent priors on the post-treatment interval effects.

Older variants remain useful as comparators and development history, but the current leading survival component is:

- spatial survival model
- no separate acute treatment coefficient
- delta-only post-treatment effects
- BYM2-style spatial frailty

### 2.6 `seer_peph.inference.run`

This module provides a thin wrapper around NumPyro MCMC.

Responsibilities:

- build and run NUTS
- store samples
- summarize posteriors
- standardize inference settings

This keeps inference logic out of notebooks and one-off scripts.

### 2.7 `scripts/`

This directory contains executable recovery and diagnostic scripts.

These scripts are used for:

- one-seed recovery
- multiseed recovery
- null recovery
- tumor-size diagnostics
- post-treatment interval composition diagnostics

The scripts are an essential part of the development workflow and should be maintained as package-level validation assets, not treated as temporary throwaway files.

---

## 3. Mathematical definition of the current survival model

## 3.1 Data structure

For each patient, survival time is expanded into piecewise-exponential intervals.

For each long-format survival row `r`, define:

- `y_r` = event indicator for the interval, taking values 0 or 1
- `e_r` = exposure time in that interval
- `k_r` = baseline survival interval index
- `k_post,r` = post-treatment interval index for treated rows
- `treated_r` = indicator that this row occurs after treatment initiation
- `a_r` = area index
- `x_r` = baseline covariate vector

The baseline covariates currently used in the leading model are:

- age per 10 years, centered
- CCI
- log tumor size
- stage II
- stage III

---

## 3.2 Piecewise-exponential likelihood

The model uses the standard Poisson representation of the piecewise-exponential survival model.

For row `r`:

y_r ~ Poisson(e_r * lambda_r)

with linear predictor

log(lambda_r) = alpha_{k_r} + x_r transpose beta + u_{a_r} + treated_r * delta_{k_post,r}

where:

- `alpha_k` is the log baseline hazard for survival interval `k`
- `beta` is the vector of baseline covariate effects
- `u_a` is the spatial frailty for area `a`
- `delta_j` is the treatment-history effect for post-treatment interval `j`

In the current delta-only formulation:

- there is no separate `beta_td`
- `delta_0` is the acute post-treatment effect
- `delta_1, delta_2, ...` are the treatment effects in later post-treatment intervals

Thus the entire post-treatment hazard effect is represented directly through the `delta` vector.

For untreated rows, `treated_r = 0`, so the treatment-history contribution is zero.

---

## 3.3 Interpretation of the delta-only treatment-history effect

Under the current model, the post-treatment effect at interval `j` is simply:

treatment effect at interval j = delta_j

This differs from the older parameterization, where the treatment effect was decomposed into:

beta_td + delta_j

with delta_0 fixed to zero.

The current parameterization was adopted because it behaved better empirically than the older decomposition.

Interpretation:

- `delta_0` = immediate post-treatment log-hazard shift
- `delta_1` = early post-treatment log-hazard shift
- `delta_2` = intermediate post-treatment log-hazard shift
- `delta_3` = later post-treatment log-hazard shift
- `delta_4` = late post-treatment log-hazard shift

depending on the post-treatment interval break definitions used in preprocessing.

---

## 3.4 Spatial frailty: BYM2-style formulation

The current survival model includes a BYM2-style spatial frailty.

Let:

u_a = tau * ( sqrt(rho) * s_a + sqrt(1 - rho) * eps_a )

where:

- `s_a` is the structured spatial effect
- `eps_a` is the unstructured area effect
- `tau` is the overall frailty scale
- `rho` is the proportion of variation assigned to the structured component

The structured effect `s` is given an ICAR-style prior through the graph Laplacian penalty.

Let `node1` and `node2` index graph edges. Then the ICAR penalty is:

sum over edges of (s_i - s_j)^2

up to graph scaling.

To identify the ICAR component, the structured effect is represented through free coordinates with a sum-to-zero constraint.

This gives the model the ability to capture:

- broad spatial smoothness
- local unstructured heterogeneity
- total area-level hazard variation

---

## 3.5 Priors in the current independent-prior delta-only model

The current working survival model uses priors of the form:

- alpha_k ~ Normal(0, 2)
- beta_j ~ Normal(0, 1)
- delta_j ~ Normal(0, 0.5)
- rho ~ Beta(0.5, 0.5)
- tau ~ HalfNormal(1)
- eps_a ~ Normal(0, 1)
- structured spatial free coordinates ~ Normal(0, 1)

The independent prior on `delta` is currently preferred to the more heavily smoothed or hierarchically constrained variants because it provided the best overall empirical balance during recovery studies.

---

## 4. Current active model choice and rationale

Several survival model variants were explored.

### 4.1 Explored variants

These included:

- survival-only nonspatial model
- spatial model with `beta_td + delta_post`
- spatial model with `treated_td` but no delta block
- spatial model with no treatment-history terms
- collapsed post-treatment interval models
- spline-based post-treatment effect
- delta-only model with iid hierarchical shrinkage
- delta-only model with centered level/shape decomposition
- delta-only model with first-order random-walk smoothing
- delta-only model with second-order random-walk smoothing

### 4.2 Current working choice

The current recommended working model is:

- spatial
- delta-only
- independent priors on `delta`

Reason:

- it gave the best overall compromise across:
  - baseline coefficient recovery
  - treatment-history recovery
  - tumor-size stability
  - sampler behavior

### 4.3 Important empirical conclusion

The development process showed that:

- the original `beta_td + delta_post` split made the model less stable
- the post-treatment block can compete with baseline prognostic effects
- `log tumor size` is especially vulnerable to that competition
- heavier smoothing or shrinkage can improve `log tumor size` recovery but often distorts the delta trajectory
- the independent-prior delta-only model is therefore the best current practical choice

This is an important modeling conclusion for future package development.

---

## 5. What the code is doing in the current active model

This section explains the main logic of the current `survival_spatial_delta_only.py`.

## 5.1 Inputs

The model reads the following arrays from `model_data.py`:

- `y_surv`
- `log_exposure_surv`
- `k_surv`
- `k_post`
- `treated_td`
- `area_id_surv`
- `X_surv`
- `node1`
- `node2`
- `scaling_factor`
- `A`

These arrays define:

- the survival likelihood
- the post-treatment effect indexing
- the spatial graph

## 5.2 Baseline hazard

The model samples a separate `alpha_k` for each survival interval.

This is the baseline piecewise-exponential hazard structure.

## 5.3 Baseline covariate effects

The model samples a coefficient vector `beta` and applies it to `X_surv`.

This captures baseline prognostic structure.

## 5.4 Post-treatment effect

The model samples `delta_post` as a vector of independent coefficients.

The effect is applied only when `treated_td = 1`.

Implementation logic:

- untreated rows get no treatment-history contribution
- treated rows use `delta_post[k_post]`

This is the simplest working treatment-history representation found so far.

## 5.5 Spatial frailty

The model samples:

- `rho`
- `tau`
- unstructured effect `eps`
- structured effect free coordinates

Then constructs:

- structured sum-to-zero field
- BYM2-style frailty `u`

This is then indexed by `area_id_surv`.

## 5.6 Likelihood

The linear predictor is:

alpha[k_surv] + X_surv beta + u[area_id_surv] + treated_td * delta_post[k_post]

and the observed event counts are modeled as Poisson with rate:

exp(log_exposure + linear predictor)

---

## 6. Summary of validation findings so far

## 6.1 What has been validated successfully

The following have been validated well:

- preprocessing pipeline
- graph construction
- simulation logic
- model-data assembly
- execution of MCMC inference
- spatial frailty recovery
- recovery of most baseline survival coefficients
- recovery of the qualitative post-treatment trajectory under the current delta-only form

## 6.2 Main unresolved issue

The main remaining issue is attenuation of the `log tumor size` coefficient in some model variants.

Current understanding:

- this is not mainly stage collinearity
- this is not mainly spatial confounding
- this is not mainly the separate `beta_td` term
- it appears to arise from competition between:
  - baseline tumor-size prognosis
  - post-treatment hazard structure
  - risk-set composition over post-treatment time

This is likely at least partly a real weak-identification issue rather than a simple coding bug.

## 6.3 Practical implication

This does not invalidate the model for its main scientific use, which is trajectory-level comparison under different treatment timing scenarios.

It mainly limits how literally one should interpret the isolated coefficient for tumor size.

---

## 7. How the current model answers the scientific question

The main scientific use is not just coefficient recovery, but prediction of survival under different treatment timings.

For a fixed individual with covariates `x` in area `a`, and a chosen treatment time `T`, the model defines a piecewise hazard trajectory.

From this, one can compute:

- survival curve under treatment at 30 days
- survival curve under treatment at 60 days
- difference in survival probability at clinically relevant horizons
- difference in restricted mean survival time

So the model is still appropriate for the core scientific aim of comparing survival under different treatment times, even if one baseline coefficient is somewhat attenuated.

---

## 8. Recommended current workflow

The recommended workflow for development and applied use is:

1. simulate data under known truth
2. preprocess with `prep.py`
3. assemble arrays with `model_data.py`
4. fit the current active survival model
5. evaluate:
   - coefficient recovery
   - treatment-history recovery
   - spatial frailty recovery
   - null calibration
   - divergence diagnostics
6. use posterior samples for counterfactual survival predictions
7. then move to joint-model development

---

## 9. Current recommended active files

### Keep as active

- `seer_peph/data/prep.py`
- `seer_peph/data/model_data.py`
- `seer_peph/graphs.py`
- `seer_peph/validation/simulate.py`
- `seer_peph/inference/run.py`
- `seer_peph/models/survival_spatial_delta_only.py`

### Keep as archived comparators

These should be preserved but clearly marked as nonprimary:

- `survival_only`
- `survival_spatial` with `beta_td + delta_post`
- spline-based post-treatment variants
- centered level/shape and RW variants if retained for sensitivity checks

These are useful for methodological comparison and reproducibility, even if they are not the current default.

---

## 10. Roadmap for next development steps

## 10.1 Immediate next step: lock the current survival component

Before building the full joint model, finalize the current survival component.

Tasks:

- keep the plain delta-only independent-prior spatial survival model as the primary survival model
- finish null-case validation
- finalize a standard recovery script set
- document the rationale for choosing this model over the explored alternatives

Deliverable:

- stable survival model interface and validation summary

---

## 10.2 Build posterior prediction utilities

The next package-level feature should be posterior prediction utilities for counterfactual survival.

Needed functionality:

- compute individual survival curves under specified treatment times
- compare treatment at 30 vs 60 days
- compute survival difference at fixed horizons
- compute restricted mean survival time difference
- summarize uncertainty across posterior draws

Deliverable:

- prediction module for model-based counterfactual survival analysis

This is directly tied to the scientific aims.

---

## 10.3 Implement the treatment-process model as a standalone module

The package still needs a proper treatment-time model.

Planned direction:

- piecewise-exponential or related hazard model for treatment initiation time
- baseline covariates
- spatial frailty
- compatibility with the current simulation and preprocessing structure

Deliverable:

- treatment-only model with its own recovery studies

This should be validated before the full joint model is built.

---

## 10.4 Build the full joint treatment-survival model

After treatment-only and survival-only components are stable, build the joint model.

The intended coupling is through shared spatial frailty first, with possible future extensions.

Planned ingredients:

- treatment hazard model
- survival hazard model
- shared latent spatial structure
- posterior sampling and prediction

Deliverable:

- first full joint model implementation

This is the major next scientific milestone.

---

## 10.5 Add posterior diagnostic utilities

The package should include reusable diagnostic tools rather than relying entirely on custom scripts.

Needed diagnostics:

- divergences
- effective sample size
- posterior correlation summaries
- coefficient path comparison across model variants
- null-case diagnostics
- recovery summaries across seeds

Deliverable:

- package-level diagnostics module

---

## 10.6 Standardize validation scripts

Current validation relies on many custom scripts. These should be standardized and retained.

Recommended script families:

- one-seed recovery
- multiseed recovery
- null multiseed recovery
- tumor-size diagnostics
- post-treatment interval composition diagnostics
- posterior-correlation diagnostics

Deliverable:

- a standard validation suite with consistent output structure

---

## 10.7 Add prediction and reporting artifacts

For applied analyses, the package should generate outputs that are immediately interpretable.

Examples:

- survival curves
- treatment-time comparison plots
- RMST difference summaries
- area-level frailty maps
- calibration figures
- posterior interval tables

Deliverable:

- reporting utilities for methods development and real-data analysis

---

## 10.8 Prepare for real Georgia analysis

Once the model is stable enough, move from simulation-only development to real-data readiness.

Needed steps:

- finalize preprocessing assumptions for SEER-Medicare CRC data
- confirm graph mapping for Georgia geography
- verify treatment-time variable construction
- run model on a controlled real-data subset
- inspect computational scaling

Deliverable:

- first real-data pilot analysis

---

## 10.9 Longer-run methodological extensions

After the core package is stable, possible extensions include:

- interaction between tumor size and treatment-history effect
- richer treatment-history bases
- alternative treatment-process hazard families
- additional outcomes
- more flexible shared latent structures
- more causal framing if assumptions can be justified

These are later-phase items, not immediate priorities.

---

## 11. Recommended project priorities right now

The highest-priority sequence is:

1. finalize and validate the current delta-only spatial survival model
2. build posterior prediction utilities
3. implement and validate the treatment-time model
4. build the full joint model
5. run real-data pilot analyses

That ordering keeps development aligned with the scientific goals and avoids overinvesting in local refinements to a survival submodel that is already adequate for its main purpose.

---

## 12. Final summary

The package has reached a stage where the survival component is substantially developed and validated.

The current best working survival model is:

- spatial
- piecewise-exponential
- delta-only treatment-history parameterization
- independent priors on delta
- BYM2 spatial frailty

This model is not perfect, but it is the best overall compromise found so far.

The main unresolved coefficient-level issue is attenuation of `log tumor size` in some variants, but this does not prevent the model from serving its main scientific purpose: comparing survival under alternative treatment timings.

The next real milestone is no longer more local survival-model tuning. It is development of:

- prediction utilities
- treatment-process modeling
- and the full joint treatment-survival model
